text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marcus T. Delgado is a 58-year-old male with a recent colonoscopic biopsy demonstrating moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon. Presenting complaint included a several-month history of intermittent hematochezia, altered bowel habits, and an unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 lbs. Presenting CEA markedly elevated at 487 ng/mL. This examination is requested for initial oncologic staging.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following intravenous administration of iohexol contrast (100 mL at 3 mL/sec) in portal venous phase acquisition. Thin-section axial reconstructions obtained at 2.5 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal reformats reviewed. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and soft tissue windows evaluated in their respective planes. No oral contrast was administered per departmental protocol. No adverse reaction to contrast.

COMPARISON:
No prior cross-sectional imaging available for comparison. This is a baseline staging examination.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations are identified. The tracheobronchial tree is patent to the subsegmental level. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The thymus is involuted and unremarkable for patient age. No mediastinal mass.

Heart and Pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The visualized portions of the aorta and great vessels are unremarkable without aneurysmal dilation or intimal irregularity.

Chest Wall and Pleura: No chest wall lesion. No pleural thickening or effusion.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is enlarged, measuring approximately 19 cm in craniocaudal span. Four discrete hepatic lesions are identified, distributed across both hepatic lobes, consistent with metastatic deposits:

  - Segment VI: A hypoattenuating, irregular-margined mass measuring 3.8 x 3.3 cm. This is the dominant hepatic lesion. It demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement on portal venous phase with a central area of low attenuation consistent with internal necrosis or cystic change. The surrounding hepatic parenchyma is mildly displaced.

  - Segment IVa: A rounded hypoattenuating lesion measuring 2.1 x 1.9 cm situated in the medial left lobe adjacent to the middle hepatic vein. Faint peripheral enhancement visible.

  - Segment II: A hypoattenuating lesion measuring 1.3 x 1.1 cm in the lateral left hepatic lobe. Well-circumscribed with no apparent communication with adjacent biliary radicles.

  - Segment VII: A smaller rounded low-density focus measuring 0.9 x 0.8 cm in the posterior right lobe. Enhancement pattern consistent with the other hepatic lesions.

No additional hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The common bile duct is normal in caliber at 4 mm. The portal vein is patent. No hepatic vein thrombosis.

Biliary System: Gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis, cholecystitis, or pericholecystic fluid.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreatic head, body, and tail are normal in contour and parenchymal density without ductal dilation or focal mass.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and normal in morphology. No adrenal nodule or thickening.

Kidneys and Collecting System: The right kidney measures 11.4 cm and the left kidney measures 11.8 cm. Normal renal cortical thickness bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi. No solid renal mass. A 5 mm simple cyst is noted in the upper pole of the right kidney, benign in appearance.

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No para-aortic or aortocaval lymph nodes exceeding 1 cm in short axis. The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber (2.1 cm) without atherosclerotic aneurysm. No mesenteric lymphadenopathy. The mesenteric fat is unremarkable without stranding remote from the primary tumor.

Stomach and Small Bowel: The stomach is unremarkable without wall thickening or luminal abnormality. The small bowel demonstrates normal wall thickness and fold pattern throughout without obstruction, dilation, or intraluminal abnormality.

---

PELVIS:

Sigmoid Colon — Primary Tumor: There is an irregular, circumferential mass-like mural thickening involving the sigmoid colon measuring 4.5 x 3.8 cm in cross-section with an estimated longitudinal extent of approximately 5 cm. The mass demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement. There is significant pericolonic fat stranding extending beyond the bowel wall, indicating transmural extension of the tumor and infiltration into the pericolic mesorectal fat. The surrounding planes are ill-defined along the anterior and posterior margins. There is no evidence of direct invasion into adjacent pelvic organs (uterus absent, bladder appears uninvolved, although the posterior bladder wall should be evaluated carefully on MRI if needed). No gross perforation.

Regional Lymph Nodes (Sigmoid Mesentery): Five regional pericolic and sigmoid mesenteric lymph nodes are enlarged and morphologically suspicious, ranging from 0.8 cm to 1.4 cm in short axis diameter. The largest node is located approximately 3 cm proximal to the primary tumor. Additional smaller nodes with round morphology (short-to-long axis ratio >0.8) in the sigmoid mesocolon are noted.

Rectum: The rectum and rectosigmoid junction appear grossly unremarkable without discrete mural thickening, though the distal sigmoid involvement is just proximal to the rectosigmoid. No rectal mass identified.

Bladder and Ureters: The urinary bladder is normally distended with smooth walls. No filling defect or mural thickening. Bilateral ureters are not dilated.

Iliac and Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No pathologically enlarged external iliac, internal iliac, or obturator lymph nodes.

Osseous Structures: The visible pelvis, sacrum, and proximal femora are unremarkable in density and morphology. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion. Degenerative changes at the sacroiliac joints are age-appropriate.

Abdominal Wall and Peritoneum: The peritoneal surfaces are smooth. No peritoneal nodularity, omental caking, or serosal implants are identified to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No free intraperitoneal fluid or ascites.

Vascular: The abdominal aorta and common/external/internal iliac arteries are patent without aneurysmal or occlusive disease. No venous thromboembolism identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Sigmoid colon primary mass measuring 4.5 x 3.8 cm with full-thickness mural involvement, pronounced pericolonic fat infiltration, and five morphologically suspicious regional lymph nodes (largest 1.4 cm), consistent with locally advanced sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma.

2. Four hepatic metastases with bilobar distribution: dominant right lobe lesion in segment VI (3.8 cm), segment IVa (2.1 cm), segment II (1.3 cm), and segment VII (0.9 cm). The bilobar distribution is noted; formal assessment of resectability and future liver remnant volume will require hepatobiliary surgery consultation and dedicated liver protocol MRI.

3. No pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusions, and no evidence of thoracic metastatic disease.

4. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

5. No evidence of osseous metastatic involvement on CT.

6. No additional extrahepatic abdominal metastatic disease identified beyond the primary sigmoid tumor and regional lymphadenopathy.

Overall findings are consistent with Stage IV colorectal cancer with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastatic involvement. Correlation with clinical, laboratory, and pathologic data is recommended. Multidisciplinary oncologic evaluation with hepatobiliary surgery is suggested regarding resectability assessment.

Reporting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist]
Dictated by: [Resident/Fellow]
Signed electronically by: [Attending Radiologist]
